meta.name: go
meta.title: Go Skill
meta.description: Instructions for writing Go code following idiomatic Go practices and community standards Triggers on: **/*.go,**/go.mod,**/go.sum

original_definition.skill_description: Instructions for writing Go code following idiomatic Go practices and community standards Triggers on: **/*.go,**/go.mod,**/go.sum

actions.name: Write simple, clear, and idiomatic Go code
actions.description: Write simple, clear, and idiomatic Go code

actions.name: Write self-documenting code with clear, descriptive names
actions.description: Write self-documenting code with clear, descriptive names

actions.name: Document exported types, functions, methods, and packages
actions.description: Document exported types, functions, methods, and packages

actions.name: Leverage the Go standard library instead of reinventing the
actions.description: Leverage the Go standard library instead of reinventing the wheel (e.g., use `strings.Builder` for string concatenation, `filepath.Join` for path cons

actions.name: Prefer standard library solutions over custom implementation
actions.description: Prefer standard library solutions over custom implementations when functionality exists

actions.name: Write comments in English by default; translate only upon us
actions.description: Write comments in English by default; translate only upon user request

actions.name: Choose names that describe what the package provides, not wh
actions.description: Choose names that describe what the package provides, not what it contains

actions.name: If you need to replace the entire file content, start with t
actions.description: If you need to replace the entire file content, start with the existing package name

actions.name: Use the SAME package name as existing files in that director
actions.description: Use the SAME package name as existing files in that directory

actions.name: If it's a new directory, use the directory name as the packa
actions.description: If it's a new directory, use the directory name as the package name

actions.name: Write exactly one `package <name>` line at the very top of t
actions.description: Write exactly one `package <name>` line at the very top of the file

actions.name: If replacing file content, include only ONE `package` declar
actions.description: If replacing file content, include only ONE `package` declaration in the new content

actions.name: Exported names start with a capital letter
actions.description: Exported names start with a capital letter

actions.name: Unexported names start with a lowercase letter
actions.description: Unexported names start with a lowercase letter

rules.description: Prefer early return over if-else chains; use `if condition { return }` pattern to avoid else blocks
Avoid using emoji in code and comments
Package names should be singular, not plural
NEVER duplicate `package` declarations - each Go file must have exactly ONE `package` line
PRESERVE the existing `package` declaration - do not add another one
BEFORE writing any code, check what package name other `.go` files in the same directory use
ALWAYS verify the target file doesn't already have a `package` declaration before adding one
NEVER create files with multiple `package` lines or duplicate declarations

directives.description: Follow idiomatic Go practices and community standards when writing Go code. These instructions are based on [Effective Go](https://go.dev/doc/effectiv
Keep the happy path left-aligned (minimize indentation)
Use mixedCaps or MixedCaps (camelCase) rather than underscores
Use single-letter variables only for very short scopes (like loop indices)
Consider using typed constants for better type safety
Strive for self-documenting code; prefer clear variable names, function names, and code structure over comments
Translate comments to other languages only upon specific user request
Wrap errors with context using `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` verb
Prefer generics over unconstrained types; when an unconstrained type is truly needed, use the predeclared alias `any` instead of `interface{}` (Go 1.1
Use pointer receivers for large structs or when you need to modify the receiver
